questions.question: Which of the following situations shows that force can change the direction of motion?

options.B: A moving bicycle turning at a corner

questions.explanation: When a bicycle turns at a corner, force changes its direction of motion.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a contact force?
questions.explanation: Friction acts only when surfaces are in contact.

questions.question: Which of the following is a non-contact force?
questions.explanation: Magnetic force acts without physical contact.

questions.question: What happens when equal forces act on an object in opposite directions?

options.C: The object remains at rest or continues moving uniformly

questions.explanation: Equal and opposite forces balance each other, resulting in no change in motion.

questions.question: Why is friction useful while walking?

options.B: It allows our feet to grip the ground

questions.explanation: Friction between our shoes and the ground prevents slipping.

questions.question: Which example demonstrates gravitational force?

options.B: A ball falling from a height

questions.explanation: Gravity pulls objects toward the Earth.

questions.question: Which factor mainly affects the amount of friction between two surfaces?
questions.explanation: Rougher surfaces create more friction.

questions.question: Which of the following forces helps a magnet pick up iron pins?
questions.explanation: Magnets exert magnetic force on magnetic materials.

questions.question: Which example shows that force can change the shape of an object?
questions.explanation: Stretching changes the shape of the rubber band.

questions.question: Which force acts between charged objects?
questions.explanation: Electrostatic force acts between electrically charged objects.

questions.question: Why do vehicles slow down when brakes are applied?
questions.explanation: Brakes increase friction between the brake pads and wheels.

questions.question: Which type of force acts when we push a shopping cart?
questions.explanation: Muscles apply force to push the cart.

questions.question: What happens to friction when surfaces become smoother?
questions.explanation: Smooth surfaces produce less friction.

questions.question: Which of the following is a contact force?
questions.explanation: Friction requires surfaces to be in contact.

questions.question: Why do astronauts feel weightless in space?

options.C: Because they are in continuous free fall

questions.explanation: Astronauts appear weightless because they are in continuous free fall around the Earth.

questions.question: Which force helps keep planets in orbit around the Sun?
questions.explanation: Gravity keeps planets moving in orbit.

questions.question: Which activity increases friction?
questions.explanation: Rough surfaces increase friction.

questions.question: Which force is responsible for objects falling to the ground?
questions.explanation: Gravity pulls objects toward the Earth.

questions.question: Which example shows electrostatic force?

options.A: A balloon sticking to a wall after rubbing

questions.explanation: Static electricity causes the balloon to stick to the wall.

questions.question: Which force acts between the tires of a car and the road?
questions.explanation: Friction between tires and the road helps vehicles move safely.

questions.question: Which factor reduces friction between surfaces?
questions.explanation: Lubricants such as oil reduce friction.

questions.question: Which force allows magnets to attract or repel each other?
questions.explanation: Magnets interact through magnetic force.

questions.question: Which example shows that force can stop a moving object?
questions.explanation: Braking applies force that stops motion.

questions.question: Which force acts between Earth and the Moon?
questions.explanation: Gravity acts between all objects with mass.

questions.question: Which example best shows balanced forces?

options.A: A book resting on a table

questions.explanation: Balanced forces act equally in opposite directions, keeping the object at rest.
